lines.text: APPENDIX
Key acronyms and what they stand for

AAV — Adeno-Associated Virus ATMP — Advanced Therapy Medicinal Product (EU) BLA — Biologics License Application CAT — Committee for Advanced Therapies (EMA) CBER — Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research (FDA) CDER — Center for Drug Evaluation and Research (FDA) CHMP — Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (EMA) CMC — Chemistry, Manufacturing, and Controls CTGTAC — Cellular, Tissue, and Gene Therapies Advisory Committee EMA — European Medicines Agency FDA — Food and Drug

Administration (US) GLP — Good Laboratory Practice GMP — Good Manufacturing Practice IND — Investigational New Drug application NDA — New Drug Application NMPA — National Medical Products Administration (China) ODAC — Oncologic Drugs Advisory Committee OTAT — Office of Tissues and Advanced Therapies (former CBER office) OTP — Office of Therapeutic Products (current CBER office, successor to OTAT) PRIME — Priority Medicines (EMA expedited program) RMAT — Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy
designation
Pipeline summary, one line

Concept → Preclinical → Pre-IND meeting (Type B) → IND filing → 30-day clock → Phase 1 (often Phase 1/2 combined for gene therapy) → Phase 2 → Phase 3 → BLA filing → FDA review (with possible advisory committee) → Approval (licensure) → Post-market commitments (registry, long-term follow-up up to 15 years for AAV).
Accelerated pathway summary, one line each
Fast Track — serious condition with unmet need; rolling review and more frequent communication.
Breakthrough Therapy — preliminary clinical evidence of substantial improvement; intensive FDA engagement.
RMAT — regenerative medicine for serious condition; flexibility on endpoints and post-approval studies; designed for cell and gene therapy.
Priority Review — review-phase shortening from 10 months to 6 months; granted at the BLA filing stage for products that would significantly improve safety or effectiveness.
Approved AAV and CRISPR gene therapies referenced in this lecture
Luxturna (voretigene neparvovec) — AAV2 — RPE65 LCA blindness — FDA approval December 2017 — first US AAV approval.
Zolgensma (onasemnogene abeparvovec) — AAV9 — SMA — FDA approval May 2019 — $2.1M list price.
Hemgenix (etranacogene dezaparvovec) — AAV5 — hemophilia B — FDA approval November 2022 — $3.5M list price.
Casgevy (exagamglogene autotemcel) — CRISPR ex vivo BCL11A — sickle cell and beta-thalassemia — FDA approval December 2023 — first CRISPR drug.
Lyfgenia (lovotibeglogene autotemcel) — lentivirus ex vivo — sickle cell — FDA approval December 2023.
Three takeaways, in plain English
Gene therapy goes through a regulated pipeline — concept, preclinical, IND, trials, BLA, approval, post-market — and each gate matters.
About 90 percent of drugs that enter Phase 1 don't reach approval.

The FDA, through CBER and OTP, has built specific accommodations for gene therapy: RMAT designation, flexible trial design, intensive guidance, and post-market registries that capture long-term outcomes.
The major approved gene therapies — Luxturna, Zolgensma, Hemgenix, Casgevy, Lyfgenia — each established a regulatory precedent that subsequent programs build on.
The system is learning, and the next decade will see platform technologies, in vivo editing, and AI-assisted trial design reshape the landscape further.
Suggested follow-up reading

FDA Guidance for Industry: Considerations for the Development of Chimeric Antigen Receptor (CAR) T Cell Products (CBER, 2024) FDA Guidance for Industry: Long-Term Follow-Up After Administration of Human Gene Therapy Products (CBER, 2020) EMA Reflection Paper on Classification of Advanced Therapy Medicinal Products (CAT) The 21st Century Cures Act (PL 114-255) — for RMAT and other expedited mechanisms The original peer-reviewed approval papers for Luxturna, Zolgensma, Hemgenix, and Casgevy The
milasen case report (NEJM, October 2019) and the FDA framework on individual-patient INDs for ASOs
